clinical_coherence.notes: 56M with progressive headache and confusion, referred as 'likely GBM.' However, clinical features reveal undiagnosed HIV/AIDS: oral thrush, generalized lymphadenopathy, 8kg weight loss, night sweats, history of unprotected sex with multiple male partners. Neurological exam shows left-sided deficits and bilateral papilledema. Truly excellent case construction.

tool_output_consistency.notes: MRI shows HOMOGENEOUS enhancement (not ring-enhancing), periventricular location with subependymal spread, and diffuse DWI restriction - all classic PCNSL features that differ from GBM. rCBV 2.4 (moderate, vs GBM >4.0). Labs reveal HIV+ with CD4 82, elevated EBV DNA. CSF shows monoclonal B-cells (CD20+/BCL6+/MUM1+). Biopsy confirms EBV-associated DLBCL. PET-CT excludes systemic lymphoma. All perfectly concordant.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD C85.10 correct for PCNSL. Differential includes GBM, CNS toxoplasmosis, abscess, metastasis with excellent distinguishing features. Critical actions correctly emphasize HIV testing, avoiding craniotomy, obtaining CSF before steroids, and stereotactic biopsy. Contraindicated actions include craniotomy/resection, pre-biopsy steroids, and empiric toxo treatment for a single mass.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Exceptional puzzle. Referred as GBM but is actually PCNSL in undiagnosed AIDS. Requires recognizing (1) systemic HIV features, (2) imaging differences between PCNSL and GBM, (3) fundamentally different management. Multiple layers of complexity.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 56M, retired postal worker, 15-year history of unprotected sex with multiple male partners, no medical care for 10+ years. Highly realistic profile for undiagnosed HIV/AIDS presenting with PCNSL.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellent red herrings: (1) Enhancing mass referred as 'likely GBM' anchors to the most common diagnosis, (2) Positive Toxoplasma IgG in AIDS patient with brain mass suggests toxoplasmosis, (3) DWI restriction could be mistaken for GBM if the reader doesn't distinguish diffuse vs rim patterns. All are realistic, clinically relevant distractors.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional teaching case. Covers PCNSL vs GBM imaging differentiation, HIV/AIDS recognition from systemic clues, EBV role in PCNSL, the 'vanishing tumor' phenomenon with steroids, CSF cytology/flow cytometry, and the critical management difference (chemotherapy not surgery). One of the strongest cases in the dataset.

strengths: Masterful case construction with undiagnosed HIV/AIDS presenting as brain mass
Excellent imaging differentiation teaching (homogeneous vs ring enhancement, moderate vs high rCBV)
Comprehensive CSF, biopsy, and PET-CT workup
Red herrings are clinically realistic and educational
Critical management differences (chemo not surgery, avoid steroids before biopsy) are high-yield
